characteristics: Comforting screens that occur when things go wrong without destroying user data.
dos: Keep all user input safe; provide a clear path to resolution and use human, positive language.
donts: Do not use 'Blue Screen of Death' style jargon; never delete user work due to a form error.

characteristics: Placing primary controls within the natural sweeping arc of a user's thumb.
dos: Place primary actions and navigation near the bottom of the screen for large mobile devices.
donts: Avoid placing critical navigation (like 'Back' or 'Purchase') in the top-left 'Ow' zone.

characteristics: Eliminating work required of the user beyond giving consent.
dos: Use intelligent defaults and automate repeated actions after initial informed consent.
donts: Don't use behind-the-scenes trickery; always ensure user consent is obtained first.

characteristics: Readability is affected by existing knowledge, titles, and text structure.
dos: Provide meaningful titles and headlines; use simple words and shorter syllables.
donts: Assume people will remember specific information just because they read it.

characteristics: Text must be large enough to read without strain; x-height affects perceived size.
dos: Use a large point size and a font with a large x-height for online viewing.
donts: Use small fonts that require users to squint, as this creates a negative mood.

characteristics: Working memory is fragile and easily disrupted by sensory input.
dos: Keep information in one place so users don't have to remember it across pages.
donts: Ask users to do another task while holding information in working memory.

principle: People remember only four items at once
characteristics: The true limit of working memory is four items, not seven.
dos: Chunk information into groups of no more than four items.
donts: List more than four items in a single group without further categorization.

principle: People are more motivated as they get closer to a goal
characteristics: The goal-gradient effect states that behavior accelerates as the goal comes into sight.
dos: Show progress bars; provide an 'illusion of progress' (e.g., a 10-stamp card with 2 pre-filled).
donts: Ignore the post-reward resetting phenomenon where motivation drops after a goal is met.

characteristics: Unpredictable rewards (variable ratio) result in the highest rate of behavior repetition.
dos: Use unpredictable rewards for maximum engagement (e.g., slot machine mechanics).
donts: Use fixed rewards if you want to maintain long-term information-seeking behavior.

characteristics: Humans 'satisfice,' meaning they look for the good-enough solution rather than the optimal one.
dos: Design for scanning; use white space and large fonts to make a task look 'easy'.
donts: Provide too many options that require heavy analysis.

characteristics: The most important actionable element tied to sales must be visually unique.
dos: Use a unique color or style; ensure high contrast against the background; use actionable wording.
donts: Do not use more than one CTA on a single page as it divides user attention and lowers conversion; do not make it too small or so big it causes 'banner blindness'.

characteristics: Placement of buttons should follow natural scanning patterns to facilitate the flow to checkout.
dos: Place buttons below descriptive elements; align them to the sides where users expect them; follow the F-pattern (top to bottom, left edge).
donts: Do not place the strongest visual element in a position that requires the eyes to travel back and forth excessively, which slows processing.

characteristics: Navigation patterns should accommodate how users physically hold and reach across mobile devices.
dos: Use bottom tab menus for high accessibility; place primary controls in areas easily accessible by the thumb.
donts: Do not place primary navigation in the top-left or top-right corners (like hamburger menus) as they are the least accessible for one-handed use.

characteristics: Each piece is part of a greater whole and should contribute positively to the system at scale. No isolated features or outliers.
dos: Ensure every component addresses all four design principles to maintain a cohesive ecosystem.
donts: Do not create isolated features or design elements that do not fit into the broader system architecture.

characteristics: Agnostic to device and accessible to all; designed for a global family across 27 languages.
dos: Consider the entire extended family of users and ensure cross-platform compatibility.
donts: Do not design for a single device type or ignore accessibility and language requirements.

characteristics: The hierarchy of priorities where clarity and efficiency always precede aesthetic concerns.
dos: Ensure the message of a talk or item is fully legible even if it requires a less 'perfect' layout.
donts: Do not clip or hide important content (like long titles) just to maintain a clean visual look.

characteristics: Clear and to the point; easy-to-follow actions that do not distract from the primary task.
dos: Use motion to simplify the user experience and respect the user's time.
donts: Do not use motion that distracts from the primary task.

characteristics: Creates a sense of efficiency and responsiveness while remaining subtle.
dos: Use for micro-interactions like button states, dropdowns, and data table rendering.
donts: Do not use dramatic or slow animations in task-heavy productivity contexts.

characteristics: Entrances should inform exits to establish spatial orientation.
dos: Animate objects back to their origin point (e.g., if it entered from bottom, exit to bottom).
donts: Do not have objects disappear in a direction unrelated to their entry, which confuses the user's mental map.

characteristics: The 'zenith' of the product's potential with maximum value and content.
dos: Start design with the ideal state to set the tone for all other states.
donts: Do not design empty or error states without first defining the ideal functional state.

characteristics: The state seen when there is no data to show (first use, cleared data, or no results).
dos: Tell the user exactly which button to press and why; use stand-in content to show potential.
donts: Avoid saying 'Nothing to see here'; do not use oppressive 'coach marks' that cover the UI.

characteristics: Designing for 'smart but busy' users and being considerate of human frailties.
dos: Focus on 'intermediates' who want to get a task done quickly without feeling like a novice.
donts: Do not force users to memorize abstract icons; use clear labels where possible.

characteristics: Reducing clutter by removing redundant labels when formats or context are clear.
dos: Use formatting (like $ or @) to imply the nature of data; combine labels into values (e.g., '12 left in stock').
donts: Don't use a 'label: value' format as a default; it gives equal emphasis to all data and creates noise.

characteristics: Ensuring text legibility over busy background images in hero sections.
dos: Add a semi-transparent overlay (dark for light text) or use a text shadow with a large blur radius.
donts: Don't slap a headline over a raw hero image without checking contrast across both light and dark areas.

characteristics: Turning empty views into opportunities for engagement and conversion.
dos: Incorporate illustrations and clear call-to-action buttons (e.g., 'Add First Item') in empty states.
donts: Don't settle for 'No items found' text; it is boring and provides no path forward for the user.

characteristics: Ensuring the product doesn't break under real-world data stress.
dos: Check designs with long blocks of text, translations, and missing or varied images.
donts: Do not only design for 'best-case' or pretty artwork that will break in production.

characteristics: Ensuring text is comfortable to read on digital screens.
dos: Use at least 12pt (16pt/1em recommended); set line spacing (leading) to 1.5x font size; keep line lengths between 45-75 characters.
donts: Avoid serif fonts for visually impaired users; don't use all-caps for long paragraphs; don't use moving or blinking text.

characteristics: Confirming user actions through visual or auditory cues.
dos: Use spinners or progress messages for loading states; use pressed states for buttons; provide feedback in multiple forms (visual + sound).
donts: Do not leave a user wondering if an app has frozen during processing.

principle: Which Attributes to Include in the List Item
characteristics: Providing enough info to decide whether to skip or explore a product.
dos: Include price, thumbnail, title/type, variations, and user ratings; add 1-3 category-specific attributes (e.g., dimensions for bags).
donts: Overload the list item with too much information; use a 'one size fits all' attribute set for all categories.

principle: Product Variations as One or Multiple List Items
characteristics: Strategy for displaying color, size, or material versions of a single product.
dos: Combine variations into a single list item for minor or non-visual differences; display separate list items for meaningful visual differences (patterns, textures).
donts: Clutter the list by showing every size or format as a separate item; fail to link variations in the database.

principle: Include Product Attributes Consistently Across List Items
characteristics: Ensuring comparability between items in the same list.
dos: Explicitly state when an attribute is unavailable rather than hiding it; harmonize measurements (e.g., all in cm).
donts: Show specs only for some products; use inconsistent labeling (e.g., 'Height' vs. 'Depth') for similar products.

principle: Product Specifications Should Be Separate Entities
characteristics: Structuring information for scannability.
dos: Distinguish product type from name using font weights or colors; use bullet points for key features.
donts: Mix brand, type, and specs into a single unstructured text blob in the title.

characteristics: Preventing detours to products that cannot be shipped or bought.
dos: Use high-contrast badges (e.g., 'Sold Out'); state when the item will be back in stock; mention pre-order options.
donts: Use small, easily overlooked text labels; hide unavailability until the user reaches the product page.

principle: Always Have Thumbnails for All List Items
characteristics: Visual anchors for product assessment.
dos: Ensure every single list item has an image; prioritize image quality.
donts: Show a 'No image available' placeholder; expect users to click items without a preview.

characteristics: Removing intermediary steps that confuse site hierarchy.
dos: Invest in better list item design and larger thumbnails instead; make the Quick View a gallery if it must exist.
donts: Place the Quick View button in the middle of the thumbnail where it causes accidental clicks; make the Quick View look exactly like a product page.

principle: Allow Users to Combine Multiple Filter Values of the Same Filter Type
characteristics: Multi-select capabilities within groups like color or brand.
dos: Use 'OR' logic for values within a type; use checkboxes to signal that multiple items can be selected.
donts: Reload the page and clear selections when a second value in the same group is clicked.

principle: Diversity-Based Relevance Should Be the Default Sort Type
characteristics: Ensuring the first page represents the full breadth of a category.
dos: Show 1-2 products of each major sub-type in the first 20 results; use a matrix of sales, ratings, and views.
donts: Sort by price by default; allow a single popular sub-brand to dominate the entire first page of results.

characteristics: Users often mistake category thumbnails for specific products.
dos: Crop thumbnails so they don't depict products in their entirety or use multi-product images to show category breadth.
donts: Don't depict a single specific product clearly, as users may think the link leads to that item only. Avoid thumbnails of products in packaging.

principle: The list layout should match the product type
characteristics: Spec-driven vs. Visually-driven products require different information density.
dos: Use grid-view for visually driven industries (apparel, furniture) to allow for large thumbnails. Use list-view for spec-driven products (electronics, appliances).
donts: Don't use small thumbnails for visually driven products, as it increases pogo-sticking.

principle: Default sort type should indicate category breadth
characteristics: Initial results shape the user's perception of category boundaries.
dos: Use a default 'Relevance' sort that combines sales data, pageviews, and product type diversity.
donts: Don't sort by 'Best Selling' by default if it clusters only one product type (e.g., all Star Wars toys) at the top.

principle: When to Use a List View a Grid View or Both
characteristics: Optimizing product presentation based on whether a purchase is spec-driven or visually-driven.
dos: Use List View for spec-driven products (electronics, appliances); use Grid View for visually-driven products (apparel, furniture); switch automatically based on context.
donts: Use a one-size-fits-all approach for mixed product catalogs; hide prices in mobile grid views.

principle: Have Hierarchy-Based Breadcrumbs in Category Product Lists
characteristics: Sense of space and site position for users traversing hierarchies.
dos: Include hierarchy-based breadcrumbs on category pages; consider 'Sideways Breadcrumbs' for sibling category navigation.
donts: Provide breadcrumbs only on product pages.
